name: Assessment Stakes and Synthetic Response Use Boundaries Baseline
summary: The updated stakes-boundary synthesis finds that acceptable synthetic-response use is strongly context-dependent. A targeted 2026-06-03 pass added direct operational state summative evidence from the New Mexico MSSA technical report: LLM-generated synthetic responses were used as limited training augmentation for some Writing automated-scoring models when high-score real examples were sparse, with staff review, PED approval, and independent model validation criteria. A second 2026-06-03 operational-practice pass adds public New Meridian and Maine evidence that simulated responses can be used in summative programs as machine-scoring QA/test-suite material for scoring rules, scanning rules, and item-authoring review. This strengthens the case for tightly bounded high-stakes support uses, but not for synthetic-only final validation, subgroup fairness evidence, cut-score evidence, or live scoring decisions. In lower-stakes formative, in-term, classroom, and research contexts, synthetic responses can support scorer-development augmentation and feedback-oriented testing more readily, but only when learner consequences, response origin, label source, comparator data, and human oversight remain explicit.
main_blockers: The first pass is targeted rather than systematic.
General assessment standards and AI-assessment guidance are contextual guardrails, not synthetic-response-specific empirical evidence.

Direct high-stakes evidence now includes one operational state summative training-augmentation report plus operational machine-scoring QA sources, but these remain narrow use cases rather than a general validation policy.
The operational QA sources concern simulated responses for rule-based machine-scoring and item-review checks, not LLM-generated response training or automated essay scorer validation.
Lower-stakes examples often support prototype development, feedback testing, or training augmentation rather than operational scoring.
No included source supports synthetic responses alone for final validation, subgroup fairness validation, cut scores, or high-stakes live scoring.
best_current_signals: The Standards for Educational and Psychological Testing frame validity evidence around intended score use and make stakes central to how much evidence and protection are needed.

The New Mexico MSSA technical report documents operational state summative automated scoring in which LLM-generated synthetic Writing responses were used to supplement sparse high-score training data after scoring-staff review and PED approval.
New Meridian's Standard Processes guidance documents simulated responses as machine-scoring test-suite cases for validating scoring and scanning rules in operational form processes.
The Spring 2024 Maine Science Assessment technical report documents simulated student responses and machine-scored output review as part of state summative item authoring/review QA.

Zhang et al. provide no-direct-consequence research evidence that GPT-generated essays can support AI essay-scoring training augmentation under held-out real-student evaluation, with explicit limits around generated-essay fidelity and human ratings.
Frohn et al. show a summative constructed-response workflow where synthetic responses are acceptable before pilot testing, but real student responses carry the pilot-validation role.
Yoo et al. show in-term AES training augmentation where synthetic essays support model development while real essays remain the validation/test evidence.
Bexte and Zesch show that even lower-consequence classroom training use still requires generated-label audit or relabeling.
Watts et al. show a lower-stakes formative feedback setting where synthetic inputs support chatbot prompt and feedback testing rather than scorer validation.

Rezayi et al. show formative professional-education scoring where Qwen-generated synthetic learner responses improve training augmentation when evaluated on real learner responses, while summative use is framed as hybrid human review.

Djagba et al. show classroom NGSS scoring augmentation where GPT-generated synthetic responses can improve rare-category F1 on held-out real responses, while the authors call for additional validity work and expert quality checks.
Wang et al. provide adjacent high-stakes spoken-assessment evidence for synthetic or mismatched off-topic cases as detection/stress-test material, not automated score validation.
Walsh et al. provide adjacent high-stakes admissions evidence for synthetic demographic analogs as fairness stress-test material, not subgroup fairness validation.
interpretive_note: This baseline answers the user's stakes question by treating acceptable use as a function of assessment purpose, learner consequence, and operational role. The synthetic-response evidence does not license a blanket acceptable-use policy. It supports a gradient: lower-stakes formative and development settings allow broader exploratory use when bounded by feedback and oversight controls; higher-stakes summative, admissions, accountability, or deployment decisions can include tightly governed training augmentation, rule-based machine-scoring QA/test suites, or stress testing, but still require real-response validation where scoring-model claims are made and stronger fairness, reliability, transparency, and monitoring evidence before operational score-use claims. A simulated-response QA test suite in a high-stakes program is operational, but it is not the same evidentiary claim as validating an automated scoring model for live decisions.
limitations: The claim does not create a policy for any testing program, district, vendor, or course.
The claim does not establish that synthetic responses can replace real student responses for final validation, subgroup fairness evidence, cut-score evidence, or live scoring decisions.
The claim does not generalize one New Mexico MSSA operational report into a broad institutional accountability or vendor policy.
The New Meridian and Maine sources support rule-based machine-scoring QA with simulated responses, not LLM-generated training or validation for automated essay scoring.
Zhang et al. supports research/development training augmentation, not high-stakes deployment policy.
Some supporting guidance sources are not specific to synthetic student responses and should remain boundary context rather than direct synthetic-response evidence.
update_notes: A later pass should search explicitly for more operational technical reports, vendor policies, state-assessment documentation, and summative/interim classroom systems that mention synthetic-response rule-QA, training, validation, monitoring, or approval.
Future evidence extraction should separate no-direct-consequence research from feedback support, classroom grades, high-stakes individual decisions, and institutional/accountability decisions.

If this baseline is published, evidence review should focus heavily on applicability_boundary and synthesis_overreach because the claim uses contextual standards guidance alongside direct synthetic-response studies.

supporting_evidence.label: Use-and-stakes validity frame
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Acceptable synthetic-response use should be interpreted through intended score use and consequence level, not only through technical realism or scorer agreement.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The 2014 testing standards tie validity to intended interpretations and uses and make high-stakes uses more demanding.
limitations: Contextual standards guidance, not synthetic-response-specific empirical evidence.

supporting_evidence.label: AI assessment guardrails
supporting_evidence.conclusion: AI-supported assessment workflows need validity, fairness, transparency, human oversight, and monitoring controls, especially when use becomes operational.
supporting_evidence.rationale: ETS responsible AI assessment guidance supplies guardrails that align with the claim's deployment-readiness limitation.
limitations: Not a synthetic-response validation threshold.

supporting_evidence.label: Lower-stakes formative context
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Formative feedback and instructional support are lower-consequence contexts than final grading, selection, or accountability decisions.
supporting_evidence.rationale: LINCS/TEAL guidance defines formative assessment around ongoing feedback, instructional adjustment, and learner self-assessment.
limitations: Does not address synthetic responses directly.

supporting_evidence.label: Operational state summative training augmentation
supporting_evidence.conclusion: A state summative automated scoring program used LLM-generated synthetic Writing responses as limited training augmentation for sparse upper score points, with review, approval, and independent model validation criteria.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The 2025 New Mexico MSSA technical report describes field-test and operational responses supplemented in some cases with synthetic responses, staff review, PED approval, and model validation criteria based on human-machine agreement, degradation, and mean score difference.
limitations: Single state-assessment technical report.
Supports training augmentation, not synthetic-only validation.
Does not provide synthetic-response subgroup fairness, cut-score, or prompt/generator details.

supporting_evidence.label: Summative operational machine-scoring QA
supporting_evidence.conclusion: In a summative operational assessment program, simulated responses can be acceptable as scoring-rule and scanning-rule QA/test-suite material without implying that synthetic responses validate live automated scores.
supporting_evidence.rationale: New Meridian's Standard Processes guidance frames simulated responses as machine-scoring test-suite cases for validating scoring and scanning rules and procedures in operational form processes.
limitations: Operational process guidance rather than empirical validation evidence.
Rule-based machine-scoring QA, not LLM-generated response training or automated essay scoring validation.
No response counts or statistical performance metrics are reported.

supporting_evidence.label: State summative item authoring review
supporting_evidence.conclusion: A state summative technical report documents simulated student responses as part of item authoring/review QA for machine-scored output.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The Spring 2024 Maine Science Assessment technical report describes simulated student responses and machine-scored output review as native to the item authoring/review process.
limitations: Bounded to rule-based machine-scorable science items in ADAM.
Does not support final validation, fairness validation, or live score-use claims.
The report does not describe how simulated responses are authored or reviewed.

supporting_evidence.label: No-direct-consequence GPT essay augmentation
supporting_evidence.conclusion: In research/development contexts with no direct learner consequence, GPT-generated essays can support AI essay-scoring training augmentation when held-out real student responses remain the evaluation basis.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Zhang et al. evaluate student-only and student-plus-GPT essay scoring models on held-out student essays and report generally comparable performance, some subgroup-discrepancy reductions, and explicit limitations around generated-essay fidelity and single available human ratings.
limitations: Research/development evidence, not operational deployment evidence.
Only one human rating is available in the public corpus.
Generated target score levels are not independently verified human scores for every synthetic essay.

supporting_evidence.label: Summative pre-pilot boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: In a summative constructed-response scoring workflow, synthetic responses are acceptable before pilot validation, not as a replacement for real pilot evidence.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Frohn et al. explicitly separate synthetic pre-pilot checks and challenge sets from later real-response pilot validation.
limitations: Framework evidence rather than one operational validation study.

supporting_evidence.label: In-term training boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: In-term or classroom AES can use synthetic responses for training augmentation when real responses remain the validation/test evidence.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Yoo et al. use synthetic CASE essays for training support while preserving real classroom essays for validation and testing.
limitations: Bounded to EFL writing and CASE-style corruption.

supporting_evidence.label: Label audit boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Even low- or no-direct-consequence training augmentation should not accept generated labels without audit.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Bexte and Zesch show generated target labels can require manual relabeling before supervised content-scoring training.
limitations: Does not address operational scoring use.

supporting_evidence.label: Formative prototype boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Synthetic inputs can support lower-stakes formative feedback prototype testing, but that is not scorer validation.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Watts et al. use synthetic student inputs to test chatbot prompt and feedback behavior in a formative scientific-argumentation support workflow.
limitations: Feedback-generation prototype testing, not automated scorer validation.

supporting_evidence.label: High-stakes stress-test boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: In an adjacent admissions setting, synthetic demographic variants are defensible as diagnostic stress tests, not as subgroup fairness validation or operational decision evidence.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Walsh et al. compare original Casper responses with synthetic demographic analogs in an early fairness pilot and preserve a sample-size caveat.
limitations: Adjacent SJT admissions context.
Early pilot, not subgroup fairness validation.

supporting_evidence.label: High-stakes detection boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Synthetic or constructed response sets can support high-stakes validity-protection detectors, but this is not the same as validating automated scores.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Wang et al. create synthetic off-topic spoken responses from mismatched operational responses to develop an off-topic detector for a high-stakes speaking assessment.
limitations: Mismatched real responses rather than LLM-generated synthetic responses.
Detection and scoring-pipeline protection rather than scorer validation.

supporting_evidence.label: Formative professional-education training boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: In a formative professional-education tool, synthetic learner responses can improve training augmentation when evaluation remains anchored in real learner responses.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Rezayi et al. show stronger binary F1 for real-plus-synthetic training than limited real-only training, while preserving real learner responses for evaluation and warning that synthetic data cannot replace real annotated data.
limitations: Formative primary context.
Summative use is framed as hybrid human review rather than autonomous scoring.

supporting_evidence.label: Classroom augmentation validity boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: In classroom/in-term automated scoring, synthetic responses can help address class imbalance and improve held-out real-response scoring metrics, but validity and expert quality checks remain necessary before stronger use claims.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Djagba et al. use GPT-generated synthetic responses for rare NGSS rubric categories and evaluate augmented models on held-out real responses, while explicitly calling for more validity work.
limitations: Conference preprint.
Does not establish grade-bearing or high-stakes classroom use.
GPT augmentation is not always the strongest augmentation method in the source.

applies_when: The question is whether synthetic student responses are acceptable in a particular assessment context, consequence level, and workflow role.

Synthetic responses are used for formative feedback development, scorer training augmentation, calibration, pre-pilot checks, stress testing, rule-based machine-scoring QA/test suites, or controlled high-stakes training augmentation with explicit response origin, label source, review/approval, and comparator boundaries.
does_not_apply_when: Synthetic responses are treated as the sole evidence for final scorer validation, deployment readiness, cut scores, subgroup fairness, live high-stakes decisions, or institutional accountability.
Rule-based machine-scoring QA evidence is treated as LLM automated scorer validation evidence.
A small set of operational reports is generalized into a blanket acceptable-use policy for all state assessments, vendors, or high-stakes programs.
The workflow does not distinguish formative feedback, classroom/in-term use, summative scoring, selection/admissions, and accountability consequences.
unknown_or_unreviewed_contexts: Grade-bearing classroom decisions where synthetic responses directly affect student grades.
Certification, licensure, placement, or promotion decisions.
Operational vendor policies for automated scoring systems using synthetic validation data.
Additional state accountability testing programs beyond the New Mexico MSSA training-augmentation case and the New Meridian/Maine machine-scoring QA cases.
